attributes.stem: An otherwise well 32-year-old woman presents with a one week history of feeling unwell with lower abdominal pain. The pain had shifted to her pelvis and become constant. On examination she looks well. Her blood pressure is 126/80 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 37.2oC. Her abdomen is soft with no localised tenderness. Rectal examination shows some tenderness anteriorly. A CT shows an 8 cm diameter pelvic abscess and no other pathology.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Examination under anaesthesia and rectal drainage of the abscess
Laparoscopic drainage of the abscess and appendicectomy
Laparotomy and open drainage of the abscess
attributes.explanation:
Until recently - and still in certain circumstances - the most appropriate and least risky plan of management would have been give IV antibiotics and observe.

As this is a large abscess, some form of drainage is required and several options are available. The approach of examination under anaesthesia and rectal drainage of the abscess has been superseded by other techniques. Laparotomy with open drainage and laparoscopic drainage were once standard approaches and still might be appropriate in the haemodynamically unstable and septic patient.

Likewise, even though the underlying cause may well have been acute appendicitis, there is no clear proof of such a diagnosis and laparoscopic drainage of the abscess with appendicectomy would not be appropriate.

With today's radiological techniques and the size of the abscess in this position, this problem should be managed by abscess aspiration under CT guidance. CT, MRI, or ultrasound guided drainage with antibiotics is the current procedure of choice with a success rate of 80-90%. Caution should be taken with regards to the aetiology of the abscess. If there is a possibility of a fistulous communication with the gut, a percutaneous approach to the problem may not be practical.
learningPoints: A pelvic abscess without complications should be managed by radiologically guided aspiration
references: Khaliq K, Nama N, Lopez RA. Pelvic Abscess. [Updated 2022 May 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545292/

Sigmon DF, Emmanuel B, Tuma F. Perianal Abscess. [Updated 2022 Jun 21]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459167/
